output_text:
Critique with concrete revisions — the section overclaims novelty, glosses the actual technical contribution, and mixes intro/method/results/future-work in a way that hurts the pitch.

## Issues, ranked by how much they'll hurt you in review

1. **"First principled treatment of synthesis-time uncertainty" is almost certainly false and reviewers will know it.** Calibration for code LLMs has been studied (e.g., execution-based confidence, MBR over sampled programs, AlphaCode-style filtering, conformal prediction for code). At minimum: token-level calibration in LMs (Desai & Durrett 2020; Kadavath et al. 2022 "know what they know") and selective prediction for code (Khoury et al., conformal-code work). Drop "first principled" — claim a *specific* gap instead.

2. **Temperature scaling is a 2017 technique (Guo et al.).** Calling a temperature-scaled head the core novelty, and then admitting it must be re-fit per task family, is essentially describing vanilla Platt/temperature scaling. The interesting question — *how do you aggregate token-level scores into a program-level confidence?* — is the part you skip. That's where your novelty has to live. Spell it out.

3. **The headline numbers are not self-interpreting.**
   - ECE 0.043 vs 0.18: over how many bins? On what dataset, with what bin scheme? Adaptive or equal-width?
   - Selective accuracy 91.2% @ 50% coverage vs 78.3%: what is the base (100%-coverage) accuracy? If base is ~85%, 91.2% at half coverage is modest; if base is ~50%, it's huge. Reviewers will assume the worst.
   - You need at least one number that anchors absolute capability, not just relative.

4. **Baselines are too weak.** "Raw softmax" and "top-k frequency" are strawmen. Add: (a) execution-feedback confidence (does it pass the visible I/O examples?), (b) MBR / self-consistency over n samples, (c) at least one prior calibration method (Platt scaling on sequence likelihood, or conformal). Without these, the ECE delta is unconvincing.

5. **"200 lines of PyTorch" is a weakness disguised as a strength.** It signals to a reviewer that the contribution is engineering, not method. Either lean into it as *deployment* contribution (and reframe the paper) or stop emphasizing line count.

6. **"Orthogonal to base model … gains will transfer cleanly as code LLMs continue to scale"** — unsupported speculation. Calibration gains routinely *don't* transfer across model scales (RLHF'd models are notoriously miscalibrated vs base models). Cut or hedge with a citation.

7. **Structure.** Paragraph 1 is the pitch. Paragraph 2 is method. Paragraph 3 is results. Paragraph 4 is back to pitch. Paragraph 5 is limitations + future work. For an intro this is fine; for a conclusion it's repetitive; for a standalone abstract it's too long. Tell me which section this is and I can tighten further — I'll assume it's the Introduction.

8. **"Specification (input-output examples plus an optional natural-language hint)"** — you haven't defined the synthesis setting. Is this PBE? PBE+NL? Pure NL? This determines who your audience is (PL/synthesis venue vs ML/NLP venue) and shapes everything.

## Suggested revision (Introduction, ~same length)

> Modern code-generation models return ranked candidates without a reliable signal of *how confident* the system is in any one program. In deployment — IDE assistants, autoformalization pipelines, agentic coding loops — this forces downstream consumers to either trust every suggestion or fall back to brittle heuristics (rank, log-probability, pass-rate on visible tests). We argue that program-level calibrated confidence is the missing primitive, and that existing approaches address it only incidentally: token-level likelihoods are poorly calibrated for sequences [cite], execution-based filtering ignores cases with no executable spec, and self-consistency over samples conflates agreement with correctness.
>
> We present **CalibSynth**, which augments a transformer code model with a program-level confidence head trained to predict empirical correctness on held-out specifications. The core technical question is how to aggregate evidence — token likelihoods, execution outcomes on visible examples, and inter-sample agreement — into a single calibrated scalar. We train this head with a temperature-scaled binary objective against ground-truth correctness labels and recalibrate per task family (Section 5).
>
> On a held-out HumanEval-style benchmark (base pass@1 of XX%), CalibSynth reaches ECE = 0.043 (15-bin equal-width) versus 0.18 for raw sequence likelihood and 0.22 for top-k frequency. Selective accuracy at 50% coverage is 91.2% vs 78.3% for the strongest baseline; against execution-feedback filtering it is XX% vs XX%. We also evaluate two stronger baselines — sequence-likelihood Platt scaling and MBR-based confidence — and report wins of XX and XX ECE points respectively.
>
> CalibSynth's contribution is the calibration *target* and *aggregation*, not the underlying scaling technique; the implementation is a thin head over a HuggingFace backbone. We see this as a step toward calibration becoming a routine component of code-generation deployments, in the same way that softmax temperature became routine for classification. Limitations and the per-task-family recalibration requirement are discussed in Section 5.

## What I changed and why

- Killed "first principled" and "novelty lies in" — replaced with a specific gap argument.
- Moved the actual technical contribution (aggregation of token likelihood + execution + agreement into one calibrated scalar) to the foreground.
- Inserted placeholders (XX) for numbers you need but didn't provide — base pass@1, ECE binning scheme, results against stronger baselines.
- Dropped the line count and the "we believe / we expect" futurology.
- Demoted temperature scaling from "the method" to "the scaling technique" and made the *target* (program-level correctness) and *aggregation* the contribution.

## Uncertainty disclosure

- I don't know your exact synthesis setting (PBE vs NL-conditioned vs both); the revision assumes mixed and you should adjust.
- The specific prior-work citations I named (Desai & Durrett, Kadavath et al., Guo et al.) are real but I didn't verify they're the strongest available — a 30-minute related-work pass before submission is warranted.
- My claim that calibration gains often don't transfer across scales is supported by the RLHF-miscalibration literature (e.g., GPT-4 system card) but the strength of that claim depends on your specific setup; treat as a flag, not a verdict.
- The placeholder numbers (XX) are placeholders, not estimates — fill from your own runs.
